title: Many-Body Quantum Chaos At All Time Scales
authors: Antonio M. García-García, Lucas Sá, Jacobus J. M. Verbaarschot, Jie-Ping Zheng
abstract: We describe the dynamics of many-body quantum chaotic systems at all time scales by studying the Green's and out-of-time order correlation (OTOC) functions of the four-body, $N$-Majorana Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev model. By combining the scramblon formalism and random-matrix-theory techniques, we obtain analytical expressions for these functions at all times. The early exponential growth of the OTOC is followed by an exponential decay at a rate governed by that of the Green's function (the real part of the leading complex Ruelle-Pollicott resonances). For late times that scale exponentially with $N$, both functions have a dip-ramp-plateau pattern for $N \mathrm{mod}8 = 2, 6$ that deviates substantially from the ergodic prediction due to local-in-energy correlations of matrix elements and eigenvalues, even after the Heisenberg time.
summary: This paper provides a complete analytical description of the dynamics of the SYK model across all time scales. It demonstrates that the evolution of quantum chaos, from initial exponential growth to late-time plateaus, is interconnected and deviates from standard random matrix theory due to energy-dependent correlations.
main_problem: Achieving a comprehensive understanding of many-body quantum chaos across all time scales, specifically bridging the gap from the early perturbative/scrambling regime to the late-time RMT/ergodic regime.
main_result: The authors obtained analytical expressions for Green's and OTOC functions at all time scales, discovering that late-time behavior deviates from standard RMT predictions due to local-in-energy correlations.
method: A combination of the scramblon formalism, Random Matrix Theory (RMT) techniques, and numerical exact diagonalization/Krylov subspace methods.
model_or_system: The q=4 Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model, specifically the N-Majorana version, in the infinite temperature limit.
key_observables: Retarded Green's function G(t), Out-of-Time-Order Correlator (OTOC) F(t), Wightman function G_W(t), and Spectral Form Factor (SFF) K(t).
important_parameters: System size N, Lyapunov exponent lambda_L, temperature beta=0, and the Ruelle-Pollicott resonances.
main_assumptions: Assumptions include locality in energy for late times, energy-independent matrix elements within specific ranges, and the use of the SYKGUE model as a comparison for RMT behavior.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares numerical ED data with various analytical fits (scramblon, Ruelle-Pollicott, power-law, RMT) for G(t) and F(t); Figure 2 shows the Wightman function and its derivative; Figure 5 shows time dependence for N=26.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of multi-scale chaos, describes the SYK model and the scramblon/RMT methods, analyzes specific temporal domains (perturbative, scrambling, power-law, RMT), and concludes with a discussion on the interconnectedness of these regimes.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a unified analytical framework for understanding how information scrambling evolves into late-time ergodic behavior and identifies specific deviations from universal RMT predictions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Time dependence of the Green’s function (left) and OTOC (right) on a log-log scale. Numerical results obtained from exact diagonalization for the N = 18, q = 4, β = 0 (black disks) SYK model are compared to numerical fits and analytical results derived below, given by solid curves as indicated in the legend of F(t), also for G(t) (see Appendix B for the fitting parameters). The inset in the right figure compares the local prediction and the SYK results on a linear scale and better shows the discrepancy of less than 4 % for t > 50. See Appendix A with a detailed comparison for a larger N = 26.

Figure 2. Left: The Wightman function GW (t) at β = 0 for an out-of-time-order source with strength s = 10−6, obtained from the SD equations on the Keldysh contour up to t = 30 with spacing ∆t = 0.04 following the methods outlined in Appendix C of Ref. [55] (black), compared to the Ansatz Eq. (6) with one fitting parameter (dark red). For large t, GW (t) ∼a exp[−2Γt] + Re(b exp[−2(Γ + iΩ)t]) shows weaker oscillations than the squared Green’s function G2(t) ∼Re(eb exp[−2(Γ + iΩ)t]) obtained by solving the SD equations. Middle: The normalized derivative exp(−λLt)dGW (t, s)/sdt, compared to the derivative of the Ansatz. The disagreement for t < 2 is an artifact due to the position of the...

Figure 4. Energy dependence of the average matrix elements |⟨k|γ0|l⟩|2 as a function of El for fixed Ek. Note the enhance- ment by a factor 2 for k = l. The diagonal matrix elements |⟨k|γ0|k⟩|2 (inset) only show a weak dependence on the energy in the bulk of the spectrum. The matrix elements for k ≈l, which give the main contribution to the Green’s function, are much larger than the SYKGUE values of (1 + δkl)/D.

Figure 5. Time dependence of the Green’s function (upper) and OTOC (lower) on a log-log scale. Numerical results ob- tained from exact diagonalization for the N = 26, q = 4, β = 0 (black disks) SYK model are compared to the analytical results and numerical fits derived in the main text, given by solid curves as indicated in the legend of the lower figure. The inset in the upper figure shows the same curves for t > 100 on a linear plot. The correction to the SYKGUE result is large, but because of the factorization assumption there is still a 10 percent discrepancy between analytical and numerical results.
